01 — About

A researcher who reads
patterns in human movement.

Hyeong-Sik Kim holds an M.S. in Biomechanics from Korea National Sport University. His research focuses on how the body adjusts joint stiffness in response to footwear changes during running.

He currently works at the Motion Innovation Center, KNSU, with interests spanning biomechanics, footwear performance, and AI-assisted movement analysis.

Outside the lab, he competes and officiates in Taekwondo Poomsae — trained to read motion before he ever measured it — and has traveled to more than 10 countries.

Biomechanics Injury Prevention Footwear Science AI in Movement Taekwondo Referee

02 — M.S. Thesis Research

How shoe cushioning rewrites
joint stiffness strategy.

"Humans change their posture in response to changing external environments to adjust the effects on the body." — Arampatzis, 2002; Devita & Skelly, 1992

Four shoe conditions (Asker C-70 to C-40 midsole hardness) were used to isolate the effect of cushioning on joint stiffness during recreational running. Softer midsoles increased ankle stiffness while leaving vertical stiffness unchanged — showing the body redistributes joint strategy locally to maintain overall stability.

Ankle Stiffness
↑
Increased to compensate as midsole hardness decreased.
Knee Stiffness
↓
Offloaded as the ankle took on a stabilizing role.
Vertical Stiffness
—
Whole-body stiffness stayed constant. Adaptation was local.

Motion Capture Video

06 — Future Research

Can two athletes performing
the same motion be told apart?

"How can referees assess differences between two players performing the same motion?"

1.What kinematic and kinetic differences exist between two players performing the same motion?
2.Do differences in joint strategies contribute to greater force production?
3.Which biomechanical variables can be used to quantify these differences?

Motion capture skeleton overlay — two performers
